items.passage: Coral reefs are vast underwater structures built by tiny animals called polyps over thousands of years. Each polyp secretes a hard skeleton of calcium carbonate, and countless skeletons slowly cement together into a solid reef. Living inside the polyps are colorful algae that supply food through photosynthesis and give reefs their brilliant colors. Reefs shelter roughly a quarter of all marine species, offering food and hiding places among their branching walls. Although they cover a small fraction of the ocean, reefs support an astonishing variety of life.

Ocean acidification occurs when seawater absorbs carbon dioxide from the atmosphere and gradually becomes more acidic. As the gas dissolves, it reacts with water to form a weak acid that lowers the ocean's pH. This chemical shift reduces the carbonate minerals that many marine creatures need to build shells and skeletons. Oysters, corals, and tiny plankton grow more slowly, and their protective shells may begin to dissolve. Although the change seems gradual, it threatens food webs that support fisheries around the world.

Creativity is the mental ability to combine familiar ideas in new ways to solve problems or make art. Psychologists often distinguish divergent thinking, which generates many possibilities, from convergent thinking, which selects the best one. Studies suggest that periods of rest and daydreaming allow the brain to form unexpected connections between stored memories. Both a relaxed mood and a rich store of knowledge appear to boost creative output in many fields. Although talent matters, researchers find that steady practice and curiosity strengthen creative thinking over time.

Ancient civilizations arose along great rivers, where fertile soil and steady water supplies could support large populations. Farmers in these valleys produced surplus grain, which freed some people to become priests, soldiers, or skilled artisans. Rulers organized labor to build irrigation canals, temples, and city walls that protected growing communities. Written records emerged to track harvests, taxes, and trade, giving officials a powerful tool for managing daily life. Although many of these societies eventually collapsed, their inventions shaped the cultures that followed them.

Rocks form through slow natural processes that constantly recycle the materials making up the Earth's crust. Molten rock from deep underground cools and hardens into solid igneous rock, either below the surface or after an eruption. Wind and water break these rocks into tiny grains, which settle in layers and cement into sedimentary rock. Intense heat and pressure can then transform buried rock into denser metamorphic forms over millions of years. Although the cycle moves slowly, it steadily reshapes the landscape over vast stretches of time.

Exoplanets are distant worlds that orbit stars far beyond the boundaries of the solar system. Because these planets appear extremely faint, astronomers rarely observe them directly and instead monitor their brighter host stars. When a planet crosses in front of its star, it blocks a measurable fraction of the starlight, producing a brief decrease in brightness. The depth and timing of that decrease reveal the planet's diameter and the duration of its orbit. Although most exoplanets appear utterly hostile, a handful might sustain liquid water.
